speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: This conversation with Rosie Hecks Clark took place at her home in Moscow on April 17, 1974. The interviewer was Sherry Fields.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Could you tell them about your trip to Idaho when you were six years old?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, we were on the train. Two girls and two boys of us. Two brothers and myself and my sister. This is the one that lives down here though. She was born out here. Burt McCully.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Your sister was?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Had a brother who was born out here. My brother died when he was 22 months old.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: When did he die?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, they thought his bow and wreath were paralyzed or locked. He was 22 months old when he died. So, then we asked the best kids. Well, we settled in. We went to our uncles, Uncle Marion Hicks. My dad's brother. And, in Farmington. And we stayed there. Dad homesteaded this 106 down deep creek. And we moved up there. First we lived in a log barn with dirt floors. That's what we had. Because it was in the summertime, Dad had to go to work. He went harvesting, you know. My mother and us kids stayed right there. And we had springs up on, I don't know what to call it, put them on. But we didn't have no beds then. We had springs. And that's those straw ticks or whatever there was, mattresses. And that's what we lived in that winter. Until we built a three room house.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you help build the house?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, us kids, we didn't. We thought we helped a lot, but we didn't have much. And we didn't go to school the first year. Of course, you didn't have no overshooting. But, boy, was the snow ever deep up there up to your hips. Okay, the snow was big. We didn't go to school the first winter we here. We had school up there. We didn't go.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Was the winters hard?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yeah, lots of snow. Not like they are now. And we didn't get to go to school. Had to walk and roads weren't broke. Probably got stuck in the snow drift. If we tried to walk. So we didn't go the first winter.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you help your mother around the house a lot?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What kind of things did you do?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, I washed dishes and I sweep floors and do things like that for them. We had one little black hen. And the boys come in the house and lay her eggs in. My mother had a little box. They had a curtain around it. And she kept towels and things. That little hen would come in crawling in that and lay her eggs. She'd get out and go to the door and what I would have to let her out. I'll never forget that little part of the van she was. Come in every day and lay that egg in the house. Had brown legumes.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What did you do as children? You and your brothers and sisters?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, we had apples for beef. We cut up these apples and we'd deliver beef to each other. That sounds silly now, but that's what we did. Oh, boy. I can remember my mother, she'd never light a lamp. We'd stand alone in the summertime and she'd say, No, let's not light no lamp. Of course, she said, If anybody comes, I think we're in a home scene and they won't stop. One time this fella come up on a horseback, just peeling me off the hill. We went right on by the house. We thought it was our cousin, Harvey Hicks. It wasn't him. I don't know who it was. We went way up the cedar. We called the cedar trees up that way. Then he turned to come back. He didn't stop. What he wanted, we didn't know. But Mom didn't say, Let's not light no lamp. It got to be about eight or eight, thirty or nine. She said, Now we better all go to bed. We'd all have to go to bed. She had little windows about like those up there in the front room. She could take them out. In the summer it'd be so warm she'd take them out. She'd put the windows back in. We'd all have to go to bed.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What did your parents farm?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, we'd have to clear the land up there where we lived. We'd have to clear the brush and burn it. They just raised gardens and things like that. Dad always worked out in the summertime. In the spring of the year he worked for farmers, different farmers. He'd take his team and go down there. Come home a Saturday evening and then go back Sunday night or Monday morning. That's the way they had him making his living.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did he log?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yes, he logged.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did he live in camps?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: No, he just lived at home.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: And came home every night?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh yeah. Well, they had sawmills right there. They had two sawmills up there on Heath Creek. They logged them right to the mill. And he broke his leg. One time a log rolled over his leg and broke it. The caper quarter. And... The caper quarter. And... Yeah. I don't... I see why he wouldn't have to come out here. I'm not taking down what you say. I'm not taking down what you say. Well, it never is. Well, I won't remember it. This is the way we exist. The only way to get our information about... They fixed their hands so they... And he put his arms around their neck, you know. And they fixed his hands so they packed them to the house and they sent them to the doctor. And he come out. And he said it wrong. His leg grew crooked. But he said the fella was drunk when he come out here. I don't know who he was. The doctor. And he said he was... Well, and he... I think he went to Spokane. I don't know the twin. And had it broke over and set again. But it never... It never did grow straight. He always walked with a limp.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you help your mother? Can?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yeah, once she can. And I can't remember her cannon very much. She would cannon later, but she can. I would always help her. And there's a fella. He used to come up there. There's a fella that tried to scare people off the claims, see. And Mama scared. The other time she seen a man come or anything. And this fella come one time. She's gonna have chicken for dinner. She went after, now you watch this chicken. When I cut his head off, see where it flops. I don't care about it. Well, he went to flop and he flopped over a log. I had to go down to get him off the log. I seen a man come up. And I went to the house as far as I could go. I said, the man's coming, Mom.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Where's he at?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: I said, he's right out there. Well, he had a little hatchet with him. And Mama, she was scared to death. I just went from him starting on chopping her heads off. I come in the house and... I stood by the cook's door. I noticed jump out the big window one time. I said, I won't do that. He says, I'll give you two pizzas to make me a cup of coffee. He said, I'll eat down here to the sawmill. I'll eat so early. He was a surveying up there is what he was doing. He had that little hatchet to chop the trees on. I cut the bark down so he could tell. And she says, I'll make you a cup of coffee. He says, it hasn't been a long since we had breakfast. I don't think it's too cold anyway. I'll warm it up. He did though. He gave her two bits of a cup of coffee. And so she didn't feel so bad then. I said, I'll always jump out this window. She says, you crazy thing. So... She says, you ain't that fellow that's up here trying to run people. No, she says, I wish I could find that fellow. She said, I'd like to run him out here.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: There was a man up there that was going around bothering farmers?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yes. Yeah. Trying to get people to go off the claims that they had filed on.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: See?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, he was trying to jump claims. But he never did come to our house that we know of. Huh. That's the way it used to go.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: How did you meet Archie?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, I guess we met at a literary. I used to have literary up there. It was Saturday night. And they'd always come up there. I guess a bunch of them down by Freeze, they'd get together and get in their buggies, wagons, or whatever they had and come to literary. They'd have programs, you know, and debate. Two sides of debate on us, whatever it was, you know.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: One time they had the debate, what's the most useful, a mop or a dishrag?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: I'm not laughing. Most of the women was on that debate. I don't remember which side won. And they'd have programs, you know, people sing songs and speak pieces and everything like that. I guess that's the way I met him. I don't know how else it was.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Silly way to meet him.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you court?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: How did that go?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, well, we'd either have to come get together Saturday nights or Sunday. Sunday, go to Sunday school or church. And maybe go for a ride in the afternoon or something like that, you know. So, he used to... I was scared I worked out on the way out in the country. It seemed way out, but that one day. For Harry Estes. And him and her are both dead now. And he used to get jealous of me. Because there was a fella up down below us. He worked up there too. And the guy... I'm not thinking about him. I can't remember now. Anyway, he tried to go with me too, but I didn't. Well, I liked him alright, but I just wanted to go with him. So, one time I remember... I can't remember who it went with. It went to a ball game. He went to the ball game, but I didn't go with him. I went with this fella wherever we went. I don't remember that one. Well, he was such a good dog.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: How did he get to be such a good dog?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: So, as he come home, he thought, well I'll drive around that way. See if I can see his horse and buggy that's the hessy place. Well, he didn't see them. Then he met us going home. As he went way around and come down this hill. Then he met us. He out spoke. We spoke. We went on. He went on. Oh, Lord.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: When you got married, did you have chivalries?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, Lord, yes.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you tell him about yours?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: That's been a long time ago.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, I'll tell you. We went over to Lainton Burden's. That old house was tore down and moved away now. The house that we lived in is tore down and moved away. We lived in as folks, see. And we walked right up. Well, they come to his folks first and chivalries. They thought we was there. Well, we was over to Burden's. Well, they walked over to Burden's and hid a big tree, a pine tree. This pine tree is still there. And they watched and waited for us to come out. When we come out, they just, they had boiler lids, they had tin cans and everything. They just banged and banged. They chivalries all the way through the field. They had a little bit of alcohol. It never hurt us, the rock in your body. Oh, Lord.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you invite them in for a treat?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, yes, we invited them in.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What kind of treats did you serve?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: I think they had cigars. Cigars, I think, because I think it's mostly men. Cigars, I think somebody served.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Where did you live after you and the archer got married?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Out there at Freeze. Freeze. The old house is torn down now. Every pine tree on the place is cut down. They had pine trees. Oh, they had beautiful pine trees. They cut them all down, tore down all the fences. They farm all that now. I've never been over there since they done all that.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What did you do there? What did the archer do for a living?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Just farm the place, just dad's place, you know.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: And you rented it?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, he just stayed there. Took care of it. I don't remember how long we lived there even.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: I don't remember.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, we moved around. We had to kill all at one time. And you worked in the mines up there.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What kind of mines?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Ore mines. You've heard about that, 90.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Some men, they got killed up there, didn't you?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, that was the mine that we worked in.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Where did you live?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Lived out of Pine Creek, what they call Pine Creek. My sister lived there. Her husband worked in the mines.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Could you tell me about when you worked in the hotel? Down here?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, it's apparently how I started. We just had moved here in this house. We used to live up on Washington, up on the very end house up there. Then we moved up here in this great big house that ran for $25 a month.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What do you use for a target?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: I think it's eight rooms. And my cousin, Mabel Crooks, told me about it.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: I said, well, why don't somebody rent this house?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, they say it's just too big. And my wife says, Mom, you get this, that's a dead ticket. You use every room, you have to just put an apple box in. I said, I'll use every room in it. Oh, that was a big house. So we rented. I didn't think it would take us $25 a month. That's the most rent we ever did pay. That's an awful lot of rent. Well, there's a big barn up there now.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What's his name?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: It's huge. Anyway, he's got a swell house up there where the barn was. We had a barn. There's an old man who had a pair of horses. He rented this barn from us. And that old horse would just stomp his feet and fight. My Lord, I thought, if he could get in there, he would get him down there and stomp him to death. Well, Charles is a crazy old horse, so that was my son. He peddled papers. I thought, well, if he's a baseball bat in here, he's all right. I mean, if it isn't there, he's off playing ball. I went and seen his bat and was there. I thought, well, I could ask if he shot him in the barn. No, he wasn't in the barn. If he gets to get mad, he fights, stomp his feet. And we had a well there, and he always watered his horses up there. And so we moved up there. Then we moved down here and then last. And the folks that lived here, the girls, she moved away. Father and mother died. And they had a son that lived in Oregon. Emmie's wife is down here. They wanted to sell him. I said, the kids doesn't know. You go to school, look in front of that house. If there's a car there, come and let me know. I'll go down and I'll see them folks.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Will they?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Every time they come home for nothing. No car there, Mom. One day, they start going, Car there, Mom, there's a car there. There's an organizer there. It must be them. Go down and see them. I said, all right, I'll go down and see them. So we come down here, and they had the furniture in here and everything. They wanted to sell it. So we bought it from them. And of course, we had our furniture up there in that house. We bought it and stacked it out in the lawn, the front lawn. I said, I'll pick out what I want, but I don't want it. So we sold it at the second hand store down here. So I did. We picked out what we wanted. And I sold everything they had in the house. That is what I didn't want to use, you know. And then later on, I bought the dining room set from, oh, shoot, they used to have a grocery store right over here where Wade's is, or used to be Wade's. And I bought the dining room set from her daughter. She moved away from here. And I sent the Montgomery Ward and got all these built -ins and put them in here. These cupboards were built in here when we come here. But these here, I said, I want some built -ins. And I'll take the measurements, and I'll send and get them to Montgomery Ward, and that's what I did. And have them put in. And I bought all the furniture in the front room.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: You made the money from parking at the hotel?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Uh -huh, hotel.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Hotel?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yeah. So, um.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: How'd you get the job there?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, the woman lived up here in the corner. She come by one day, she went home.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: If I wouldn't want to work at the hotel, my old wife and I, gosh, I couldn't work at the hotel, but why don't you see why you can't?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: She said, I worked there. And she said, they need somebody off of that to work in the pantry. I said, well, I guess I could go and try it. I thought I could go try it, I guess. So I went down there and tried it, and it's alright. So Elsie said, well, she just thought it was great that I worked down there.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: This is Elsie Nelson?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Uh -huh.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: You know her? Do you know Elsie?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: No, but we've talked. Sam and Laura's talked to her. Oh. I don't know her. Well, she's an awful nice woman. She just had a fit every time I wanted to get off. But the only thing I didn't like about it, every Thanksgiving, every Christmas, well, they'd meet out at the violence, my oldest daughter. Well, I couldn't go to Elsie's to do work, see. Well, I worked there, usually I don't think of Thanksgiving or Christmas, whenever I get through, why they just, some of them come down and get me, and I can go out there too.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: I just like to work down there.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: You worked there for 22 years?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: 22 years, uh -huh. That's a long time. That's a long time, working one place out there. Made all kinds of salads. Well, Christ here, I don't know if you know Christ here or not. Yeah, it was Christ here. She lives out there close to my daughter that works up to home. Her and her boyfriend come in one time, and they, in the middle afternoon, they ordered a salad, a meat -meats -a -salad. Well, they both had to come out in the kitchen and thank me for their wonderful salad.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Could you tell what the town used to look like when you first came out here?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well...

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What the sidewalks were like and the roads were like?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: A lot of sidewalks are board sidewalks.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Were they high up off the ground?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, some of them were. That's where they had to be, you know, and some of them were low. And every Sunday, that's when they first come to town, that's where I worked out at the hotel. The kids and I would just roam this town, see, we couldn't find any of the houses. And we could always find an awful lot of them. Heaven sakes, when we first come here, we only paid $15 a month for rent for a five -room house over on Asbury Street. Good Lord, that's awful high. For a rent, you know. Well, good Lord, nowadays, you'd find a house only for less than $200, $250 a month. We'd go hunt empty houses. None of them was locked, you'd just open the door and walk in.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What did you want to move into town for?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well... We moved down below Colfax. Worked down there for a fella. I forget what his name was now. They had two boys. Michael and Timothy. I said they had a girl they called Cobra. They had two little boys. And I said, let's go to Pullman. So, all right, we'd go to Pullman. I didn't like to work for this fella very well anyway. He ranched down there, farmed.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: You had a favorite stand?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: So we went to Pullman, couldn't find a house. I said, let's go to Moscow. I said, we don't like Pullman anyway. What we'd do when we were at Moscow. And the fella we rented from, we knowed him. We knowed him when he lived in the country. And he was like, shoot, I can't think of his name now. Burt somebody. But he's dead and gone now. But anyway, he said, we've got just one house left. He said, all that got left now is over on Asbury Street. And they asked for $15 a month, five room house. So we went and looked at it. And we took it. Well, he... And then Iva, she worked in the bees over here. She got on the bees. And so P -Dust kind of got her. She calls, calls, calls. So she went to babysitting. And she worked, the last place she worked was laundry. Down here's laundry. She just worked everything, everything on the laundry.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Why didn't you want to live out in the country?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, their work was here.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Aren't you working town?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yeah, you're working town too. And Iva working town. Daughter that died of cancer. And she passed away on Ronnie's birthday, the 24th of March.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: So that's the reason we come to town. We've been here ever since.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What'd you do out on the farm when you lived out here?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: He worked for farmers. He worked.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What'd you do?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: I helped this woman that he worked for. Her husband, I'd help her. She'd have men to cook for and I'd go down and help her. Get the meals and all that. And do the dishes. So that's where Ronnie started the school. Was down in Pullman. No, Colfax case. But we moved to town right away so there was anybody up here. And he was 38 years old, 24th of March.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, my birthday party. I was up to Spokane on his birthday. And Miss Don, they lived together, him and Ronnie. Don Hill. And he bakes him an angel food cake and decorates all up. Ronnie and I went up to Lloyd Lucille Sheahan, that's the cousin by the name of Miss Spokane. Ronnie says, I think Don likes to be alone when he does his baking. He bakes bread and he bakes cookies and he bakes that cake, angel food cake, for Ronnie's birthday. And when we got home, he had all decorated up for him. Happy birthday, Ron.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: I said, oh my, look at that, I could swear on a cake, would you?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: And they had chiffon ice cream.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you ever eat chiffon ice cream?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, I thought it was good. Cake and ice cream they had. He invited some of the people that rents from up there, you know. And Don had his mother over, she lives in Spokane. And that's Freda P. She lives in Spokane. She's two years old. And you wouldn't believe it to see her get around. She's real slim and she needs to get around better than I can.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Could you tell me about the Depression days?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, it's just a skimp on this, a skimp on that. Everything that you got, you had a skimp on.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Do you think it was harder other places than it was here?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, I think it was. The way you read the paper, you don't see what it was. And everything like that, so I don't know.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Well, why did they all go through there? Why do you think they all went through there?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: They had big boxes, you know, big boxes. Packed them in big boxes and shipped them on the train. And when we got down to Depot at Farmington, that's where we got off at. Well, all our boxes were there waiting for us.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What was the train ride like?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, it was something like a bus, only the... It's more comfortable than the bus as you know. How long did it take to come from... I think it was four days and four nights. You came from... Missouri. Out from Farmington, Washington.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you meet a lot of interesting people?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, I don't remember. I was only six years old, so I don't... I just don't remember.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What did you do after you got into Farmington?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: We went to our Uncle Marion. Uncle Marion Hicks was my dad's father, or dad's brother. And we stayed there for, oh I guess, a day or two or something like that. And then they went after Deep Creek. That's where they took their claims. My uncle and my dad.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Do you know how they went about getting their claims?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, no, I don't. I don't remember. You just heard about them, so they both went up there. And I don't remember even how they went. I suppose they had teas and boogies or something like that to go in and have cars them days.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did your uncle live by you?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Get home soon next to you or something?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yeah, pretty close to us. And my Aunt Sibley, I'll never forget, she used to come over long, over lived up to a long, long time. Oh, she said, Lloyd, that was my mother's name.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: You know, Lord, what I'd like to hear right now, Mom says, no, what is it?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: No coyote to hover. Oh, good heavens, Mom says, I don't like to hear coyotes. Makes me homesick. She said, when you're home, she says, yes, it does. She said, I'd just love to get a coyote.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Is there any Indians around? Was that your pet or was that another one?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: If there was, we didn't know anything about them.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: No one's pet?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: No Indians.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What happened to your pet?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: My pet. Right here. I had to go to the public.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Well, could you tell me how you made butter?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Just with a, like I said, a churn. Put the churn, churn it up and down.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What did, was it?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: A stone churn, an old three or four gallon churn, you know. And have a dasher, churned up and down, just churned up and down. Didn't take very long to churn it. Then I got soy. I bought me a glass churn. You could just turn it around like this, you know. Screwed lid on the churn. You never did. My daughter out here's got one. Really. And you just turn it around, you just watch it, make a little butter, you know. Glass churning right through it. Boy, that was fun.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you guys sell it?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: No, we just eat it ourselves. We never sell anything.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you sell anything off the farm?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: No, I don't think we did.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: From running or anything?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: No. We had our own chickens and our own cow. Have our own milk and butter and cream. Had a separator, separated the milk. And then, um, that was just it. Once in a while we take our desert two eggs to town.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: How far did you live from town?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: About fifteen miles.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you walk in or did you go to the other side?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: No, had to go to the buggy. That was a time. People sure was. I remember one time my mother and I went to Proust. I've always drove down by the Proust River, you know. Unhitched the horses, led them down to the Proust River and watered them. And I had worked out and I had twelve dollars, I think.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: And I led one horse and she led one. I just looked down and there was a dollar bill laying right there. She took one more step, the horse, she would have stepped right over it. I was so tickled I found that dollar, that made me thirteen dollars. That was a lot of money in those days. It was, it sure was. And I told mom about it. I said, then I bought some goods, some white goods to make me a fourth of July dress. You could get it then, it wasn't that cheap. I went in the store and bought enough goods to make me a dress. And there used to be an old peddler come up to Deep Creek and he'd peddle all kinds of goods. Every summer he would come up there and we'd buy goods to make a fourth of July dress, see.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What did you do on the fourth of July celebration?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, they'd celebrate two and three days up there at the creek. They'd have a big swing. They'd put a big swing up, you know. And they'd have races and all, they'd just have everything like that up there, right. It was a big schoolhouse. They'd clear out the places, you know, and people would bring their lunches and put big lunches out, have big lunches and just really have a big time. Celebrate two and three days. Sounds like a lot of fun. Oh, it was a lot of fun. Now then, nobody would think about it, do anything like that.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: How'd you make your dress?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, we had a pattern, cut it out, we had a sewing machine. I had to do all the sewing. I'd have sewn myself and my mother and my two sisters.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: How long were you doing for the sport?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: I'd just cut them out and sit down and make them.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you have the treadle machine?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yeah. You'd sit down and sew them. So you did quite a bit of sewing. Yeah. And even after I was married, I had the two girls. The oldest girls I sewed for myself and my two girls and my mother and my sisters. They'd bring their goods down and, Mama, she didn't like to sew anyway, so she always brings them down to me. I liked to sew, I just loved to sew.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: My sis, we were talking about this, they said, you remember how you used to sew for us?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: And I said, Lord, yes. I said, I just loved to sew. I just said, get in and make them dresses and just do some happy over it.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Could you tell me about the holidays and how you celebrated them? Like Christmas, Easter?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, we just celebrated just about like we do now, I guess. Easter.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: And Christmas? Did you go into town and do shopping? Did you make things?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: We made things. We made things. We made an awful lot of things.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Like what?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, just like stuffed animals and stuff like that for the little kids and that's the way we done it.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Was it a big event, Christmas?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, not too much. It wasn't so big. Everyone was pretty poor.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Everyone was pretty poor through the Depression. Yeah, that's right. That's right. Couldn't buy much. I never will forget one Christmas, my mother bought me a picture album. It says, Our Favorites. It's about wore out. And I'm pretty sure, no, it's Hazel. She says, Mama, you're through with that. I want that. It's just about, well, the velvet part, bag was velvet, you know, red velvet. It's just about wore off. She says, I want that. Well, I said, you can have it any time you want, Hazel. She says, you don't see that many more. I said, well, I guess maybe you don't. Because I had to think that was long before I was married. I don't know, she'd always managed to get around and buy us kids something, you know.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: How often did you come into town?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Golly, about once. About once a year, I guess.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What did you get when you came into town?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, we'd get you something we had to have.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What, a year?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yeah. Well, we never went to town very much. Well, it was so far, and you're going and wagging a hack. I called them hack family. I'd go in there, well, take you all day to get there, all day to come back. We'd take her lunch. We'd go in the back of the store and eat her lunch. We'd just spend the whole day. Then we'd go home, feel so happy because we got to go to town.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Was there ever any murders in town?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: We never heard of them.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Nowadays it seemed like every time you turn around somebody's getting murdered or something. It's awful, I think.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you tell them about your school you went to?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: There was one room in the schoolhouse up on Deep Creek. And the old schoolhouse is still there. They moved it down on the creek, though, and they don't have school anymore. They've got another schoolhouse. I'll never forget one time. We lived about a mile, I guess a mile and a quarter from the schoolhouse. And us kids always had to walk. And this was on a Saturday or Sunday I was going, I don't know, I was going down to one of the neighbors, I guess. And our neighbors, they were bicyclers and leischers, they were Germans. And they had an old bull and he got out of the pasture. I thought, dear God, that looks like that old bull down there in the brush. And it was, he was right beside them. Lord, I thought, I don't know whether to go on or whether to go crawl in the schoolhouse. The schoolhouse wasn't locked, I know she's going to crawl in the schoolhouse. But I stood there and waited for a while and soon he went on around through the pasture. I went on down the road and he could have seen me, run after me then if he'd wanted to, but he didn't. I guess the Lord was with me, I don't know what else. But Bertie, my sister, one time said, she said one time that something followed me. When I'd stop walking, see we lived off on the main highway, the main road, and it went up about a mile. And we turned off another road, we put a quarter of a mile to our house. And Bertie said, every time I'd stop this, whatever it was, I think it was a coyote, I thought of a wildcat's where I thought of it. She said, every time I'd stop it, it's a... Lord, I said, I'd been run by somebody. I said, I wasn't walking very slow, so I'm telling you that, I'm going pretty fast. But I'd hear the brush cracking, brush cracking, and I'd stop and it'd stop. So, if I might start on it, it would start on. One time, I'll never forget, I worked out this esky and I got a ride up partway home. I guess two or three miles from home. So, well, it was John Viseger, he always trying to go with me. I never did like him, big old fat guy. I don't know how he know I got a ride up partway, but I was walking up home about two or three miles. I had to walk. So, he was down at the big gate, he wanted to walk home. I said, no, I said, don't take me home. It's been raining too, it's been raining. When I went up home, there was nobody home, the house was locked. I thought, oh my God, I got to get back down that road. And as I turned off, not the main highway, but the next highway, went across the bridge, just to the coyotes, standing right there. And the water was ice -cold, he smelled a puddle of water. I thought, wow. But I just kept on going, that coyote turned and went in the brush. I thought, oh my God, I got to go back down that road or if I meet that coyote. Got to go over to my aunt's, I can't stay here, can't get in. And I thought, I got to go because it's getting dark. And I went down the road, but I didn't see him, but I heard him. And he was up on the side of the hill there, cracking the brush, I could hear him. Boy, was he a big coyote. I tell you, I made tracks. I used to go to walk, I could just out walk anybody, I guess. I just took down that road and went over there and had him, he was home. I told him, folks I know, I said, he went down to Uncle Albert's, I guess. I was down with Steptoe. And they went to the team wagon. They locked the house, of course they didn't know I was coming home or anything, but I... Boy.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Sounds pretty exciting. Boy. Scary but exciting. Well, I remember one time, Dad used to get up early in the morning and go down to the meal for breakfast. He worked down there, see. And he got up and oh, the snow was just, oh it just froze, it just crusted, it'd hold up anything. And he seen a bobcat.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: He said, walking right under the snow, just right by the house there, just coming around, he said, don't any of you kids go out?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: He said, I seen a bobcat this morning out here. Boy, this kid is scared half to death to go outside.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: This is your father?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: So what'd he do?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: He just went on down to the meal. This bobcat was just passing through it, kid. He just never paid any attention to it at all.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Yeah?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yeah, just like I am with you. Just like I am with you, they're setting down stuff too. Oh my God. I couldn't go crazy, man. I'm all over the country. I don't want to have to go crazy. I know you're from a town down there, Dad.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: I'm a fat fool, you know what I mean?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yeah. But like I tell my sis, I said, Bertie, we've lived in a three room house for a long time. And Mom just had a wooden button on the door, you know, that's all kind of locks you had on the door. No screen on the door, you know.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: I said, anything is wanted in, could you push on that door?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: And they'd got in.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Bertie said, don't I know it, sis?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: I said, we got, we's all safe. Get that locked here during the door, that'd be safe. Good Lord, I said, when we got eight out. And one time, one Fourth of July, and Mama's brother and sister would come up, celebrate the Fourth.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: They'd say, all right, well, Mama just dragged off a straw tech, wasn't it, feather techs or mattresses?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Straw techs would be on the floor, make beds on the floor for the kids. They said, somebody come and peeked in the front room window, just like this, digging in the window. We'd met us kids and we'd all sleep on the floor.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Well, what did he do? Why didn't they stay in the house? This is out in the country?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yes.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What did they have to do?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Boy. Oh, Lord. They were all just trying to get in the car.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Were you the oldest in the family?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you have to help your mother quite a lot with the children?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yes, I had to help her quite a lot.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: I remember one time, my youngest brother, he was all talking about making a certain kind of pie. He's eating dinner.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: He says, how about making a pea pie?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: I felt like slapping him. Now he'd laugh, how he'd laugh.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, no.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you go to church on Sundays?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What kind of church was it?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: United Brother.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did they have social activities going on at the church?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Not very much. They'd have prayer meetings at night. Usually on Sunday night. That's the rule. On Wednesday night, they'd have prayer meetings.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did the whole family go?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Uh huh. That's the rule I would. One time, I know we went down the... We had a kind of a trail through the woods. Mom says, we'll take the lantern down and put it behind a log. So when we come back, it'll be dark and we'll get that lantern and light it so we can see where we're going. Lord, I thought... So we went back at the house in kind of a trail. We went back there. We found a big log. We set the lantern down there and went down right by the sawmill. We come out right by the sawmill. There's lots of them closer to going through the woods, you know, and go right by the sawmill. Well, when we come back... Well, it was kind of... the moon was kind of shining where the mill was. We could see a lot of men standing up there. Gosh, I said, look at those men now. I don't want to go by there and be blamed on men. Oh, Mom said, they won't hurt you. Just walk on by them. So we kept on going, kept on going. I don't remember what it seemed like to have a horse that somebody had come up after a load of wood. They had two or four, six horses on a wagon to bring back logs of wood, you know. Well, they drove them too fast or something. One horse got sick. Well, we had him laying out and had a blanket over it. Of course, after we found out what was wrong, while we wasn't scared, we went on. Well, Mom said, now, get up here. We'll get the lantern. We liked it. Because it was dark going back home. So get up. We found the lantern. We made it and went back home.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What kind of tourist did you have, Johnnie? Farm?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, we just had to feed the chickens.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: And go after the cow. We had a cow. If you turned down the wood, you'd have to go get her. Drive her home. Dad usually had the horses with him. If he was working, he'd have the horses with him. So we just had the cow to go to. We'd have to go out in the woods and have a bell on them so we'd know where to find them. And have the chickens to feed and have the eggs and things like that.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Was the days really long?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Well, it didn't seem like it. It wouldn't now, I suppose. I had to do things like that now. But it didn't seem then because we're just used to things like that.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: So...

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you go to bed pretty early at night?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yeah. Soon it got dark. Good and dark. We'd go to bed. Okay. Well, it was still dark. Yeah. That's right. We didn't have no radios or televisions or nothing like that then, even.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: You see? Well, what I think we did we do anyway? Well, we just didn't do any other pay checkers or something like that, you know?

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: How did you get your grain thumb?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: I don't know. All these plants. One up there is just quite gloomy. You see part of it up there. And the other one is... One up there's got some buds coming on it. I don't know what.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you used to work in the garden quite a bit?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, yeah. Well, when we lived in the country, all he would do was plow the garden and get it ready. I had to do all the planting and all the weeding. Oh, boy, was that a job. Gosh, I'll say it was. Cut all the kraut and make all the cabbage. I mean, cut all the cabbage and make all the kraut. That's what I had to do.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Sour kraut?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: How did you make sour kraut?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: We have a cutter. Krout cutter. Just cut your cabbage, cut it and salt it down and put a weight on it.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: What kind of a weight? Like a something really heavy?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: I would eat the pan and then fill a glass jar with water and set it on this pan. It will fold it down until it works. Yeah. I used to make all the kraut.

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Did you just make pickles? Bread and butter pickles?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Oh, Lord, yes. I wish I had a dollar for every part of pickles I made. Bread and butter pickles and everything you could think of. I used to take, oh, Bertie and my sister used to like them so well, cauliflower and cook it. Put it with my pickles. Make, oh, whatever you used to call them. Put mustard in something like that. I can't remember because I don't make them anymore. But anyway, I used to make them just, well, I used to make dill pickle 40 and 50 quart. And we lived out in the country. Those kids, how they loved dill pickles, they just loved them.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Baked all my own bread out in the country and we all my own butter.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: That's what we need now, the parts of the food. Yes, I should say so.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Some people, Lord, wouldn't know how to bake bread and make the butter, would they?

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Some, all they ever did do is go to store and buy it. It's getting pretty expensive.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: People sense a love for bread and butter. Yeah, I read in the paper though where they said that bread wasn't going to go to a dollar a loaf now because they said there was lots of flour. I don't think they just do that to raise the price of everything.

speaker: Rosie Hecks Clark
words: Is that thing running?

speaker: Sherrie Fields
words: Yes, it's running.
